title: Jeddah Preview: The Walls Are Close, the Championship Is Closer
meta: Published: 24 April 2026 | By Bernard “Bernie” Fletcher, Veteran Correspondent | F1CC News
caption: The Jeddah Corniche Circuit awaits — fast, narrow, and ready to punish any mistake.
body:
It’s 2026 and we’re heading to Jeddah.

The Saudi Arabian Grand Prix is the sixth round of a season that already feels like it’s been running for six months. Ferrari led. Then Williams led. Joshua Nathanial won a race. Jack Tef stood on a podium. The competitive order has been shuffled so many times the paddock is dizzy. And we haven’t even reached the European leg yet.

Jeddah is a circuit that doesn’t forgive. High speeds, concrete walls, and a layout that rewards bravery and punishes hesitation. The drivers who thrive here are the ones who can flirt with disaster without ever committing to it. It’s fast, it’s narrow, and it’s a test of nerve as much as machinery. The average speed is the highest of any street circuit — over 250 km/h. The walls are close enough to touch. One mistake and you’re in the barrier. One safety car and the strategy resets. The drivers know this. The engineers know this. The fans love it.

The track runs along the Red Sea coast. The floodlights reflect off the water. It looks beautiful on television and terrifying from the cockpit. There are long straights, sweeping curves, and a first sector that strings together a sequence of kinks where precision is everything. Sector two opens up, then tightens again through the final section. Overtaking is possible but not easy. DRS will help. The walls won’t.

Ferrari arrived in Bahrain with no upgrades and left with no points. That’s not a setback. That’s a warning. Eetu Väisänen still leads the championship, but his margin has been cut to 12 points over Eren Aygen. The Finn called the upgrade packages other teams brought “huge” — a rare admission of vulnerability from a driver who usually gives the paddock nothing. They’ll have new parts here. They have to. The championship cannot afford another Bahrain. Väisänen’s early-season dominance, built on rain and strategy, now faces the relentless, dry demands of a street circuit where aerodynamic efficiency is everything. Ferrari’s car has been quick but not invincible. Jeddah will expose any weaknesses in high-speed cornering. The team knows this. The pressure is on.

Williams lead the constructors’ championship. A sentence that still looks wrong no matter how many times I type it. Aygen won in Bahrain. Tef, the team principal who stepped back into the car for an injured driver, finished third. The upgrades they brought to Bahrain — chassis, sidepods, suspension — transformed the car. They went from a podium in Japan to a 2‑3 at Bahrain. That’s not a fluke. That’s a step. The question now is whether that pace translates to a completely different circuit. Jeddah is not Sakhir. The corners are faster, the walls are closer, and the margin between a quick lap and a wrecked car is measured in inches. Williams haven’t faced a street circuit yet this season. How the car handles the bumps, the braking zones, the high-speed changes of direction — we’ll find out. Aygen has been the most consistent driver this season behind Väisänen. Tef’s return was a feel‑good story, but he’s a team principal, not a full‑time driver. Lucas Koopman is still recovering from his back injury. The team will need Aygen to keep delivering.

The driver swap between Riley Swansson and Afonso Cachinho continues. Swansson scored points on his Ferrari debut in Bahrain. Ninth place, from thirteenth on the grid. Clean race, no mistakes. Cachinho finished P17 for Haas. The arrangement is temporary, but the contrast is already stark. A good weekend for Swansson in Jeddah, and the pressure to make the change permanent will intensify. Ferrari haven’t committed either way, but the second seat has become the paddock’s favourite conversation. Swansson has the chance to make himself indispensable. Cachinho has the chance to prove he still belongs. Two races. The clock is ticking.

On the track, the racing has been unpredictable. Audi won in Japan, then struggled in Bahrain. Robin Keisner’s victory at Suzuka felt like the start of something. A pit‑stop issue and a collision with Maikel Zwart in Bahrain made it feel like a false dawn. Audi are fourth in the constructors’. The car is quick but fragile. The team is still learning. Jeddah will test their operational sharpness. Keisner has the pace. He needs a clean weekend.

McLaren’s Joshua Nathanial went from three DNFs to a victory in Bahrain. His season has been a rollercoaster. He qualifies well, races aggressively, and either breaks down or wins. There’s no middle ground. Zhao Lei, in the other McLaren, has been quietly accumulating points. The team is third in the constructors’. Nathanial’s win proved the car is quick. Now he has to prove it wasn’t a one‑off.

Mercedes’ troubles are still bubbling. Zwart’s collision with Keisner in Bahrain was another chapter in a difficult season. Vandesteene’s early‑season podium in Australia feels like a distant memory. The engine upgrade brought to Japan showed promise, but the car isn’t where it needs to be. They’re fifth in the constructors’, behind Williams, Ferrari, McLaren, and Audi. That’s not acceptable for a team of Mercedes’ resources. Jeddah’s high‑speed nature will expose any lingering weaknesses in the power unit. Zwart is frustrated. The team is under pressure. The season is slipping away.

Red Bull have been anonymous. Jason Roy has scored points consistently, but the team is seventh in the constructors’. Verhaeren has a sprint win but hasn’t finished in the points consistently. The car is fast in a straight line but unstable under braking. Jeddah demands stability. The team knows this. Racing Bulls, their sister team, are sixth — one place ahead. The two‑time defending constructors’ champions are now sixth. That fall has been steep, and the faces in the garage show it. Kevu has been grinding out results. Wright has been lost in the midfield. The car isn’t the force it was.

The championship is tighter than anyone predicted. Väisänen leads with 64 points. Aygen is second on 52. Keisner third on 41. Vandesteene and MaraDöner are tied on 37. Kevu is sixth on 34. Lei seventh with 31. Roy eighth on 27. Nathanial ninth on 25. Springfield tenth on 24. That’s ten drivers within 40 points of the lead. The constructors’ fight is equally close. Williams 70, Ferrari 66, McLaren 56, Audi 50, Mercedes 49, VCARB 48. Every point matters. Jeddah could scramble the order again.

The grand prix runs on Sunday, 26 April. The Corniche Circuit will be bathed in floodlights, the walls glittering under the lights. It’s a circuit that looks beautiful on television and terrifying from the cockpit. The drivers will hold their breath through the fast left‑right sequence, brush the barriers, and hope the car holds together. The championship is tighter than anyone predicted. The walls are waiting.

On to the next one, then.
